blocks:
Ayahs

وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ (١)
Woe to every scandal-monger and slanderer, (1)
ٱلَّذِى جَمَعَ مَالًا وَعَدَّدَهُۥ (٢)
who amasses wealth and counts it over. (2)
يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مَالَهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَهُۥ (٣)
He supposes his wealth will make him immortal! (3)
كَلَّا ۖ لَيُنۢبَذَنَّ فِى ٱلْحُطَمَةِ (٤)
No indeed! He will surely be cast into the Crusher. (4)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحُطَمَةُ (٥)
And what will show you what is the Crusher? (5)
نَارُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُوقَدَةُ (٦)
[It is] the fire of Allah, set ablaze, (6)
ٱلَّتِى تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى ٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةِ (٧)
which will overspread the hearts. (7)
إِنَّهَا عَلَيْهِم مُّؤْصَدَةٌ (٨)
Indeed it will close in upon them (8)
فِى عَمَدٍ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ (٩)
in outstretched columns. (9)

(Commentary)

This is a severe threat directed at those who are infatuated with amassing wealth, using it to exalt themselves over people and behaving arrogantly toward them. Consequently, they look down upon others and find fault in them for things that are not truly faults. The Surah is Meccan.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيْلٌ لِكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُمَزَةٍ "Woe to every slanderer and backbiter!" (104:1). It is stated in al-Majma‘: The slanderer (الهمزة) is one who frequently stabs at the character of others without right, finding fault in them for what is not a fault; the root of al-hamz (الهمز) is to break. He said: al-lamz (اللمز) also means finding fault, so al-humazah (الهمزة) and al-lumazah (اللمزة) carry the same meaning. It has also been said that there is a difference between them: the humazah is one who slanders you behind your back (بظهر الغيب), while the lumazah is one who slanders you to your face; this is narrated from al-Layth.

It is also said: the humazah is one who hurts his companion with foul language, while the lumazah is one who narrows his eyes at his companion, gestures with his head, or signals with his eyes. He said: the form of the word (فعله) is a construction of intensive exaggeration (بناء المبالغة) describing one who performs the act frequently until it becomes a habit for him. You say: a man is nukahah (نكحة) if he marries frequently, or duhakah (ضحكة) if he laughs frequently; likewise are humazah and lumazah. End of quote.

Thus, the meaning is: woe to every fault-finder and backbiter. It has been interpreted with other meanings based on the differences in defining al-humazah and al-lumazah.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي جَمَعَ مالاً وَعَدَّدَهُ يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مالَهُ أَخْلَدَهُ "who gathers wealth and counts it [repeatedly]. He supposes that his wealth will make him immortal." (104:2-3). This is an exposition (بيان) of the slanderer and backbiter. The use of the indefinite form for "wealth" (مالاً) is for disparagement (التحقير); for wealth, no matter how much it increases, does not avail its owner anything except what he spends on his natural needs, such as a meal that satiates him or a drink of water that quenches his thirst, and the like. "And counts it" (وَعَدَّدَهُ) is from al-‘add (العد), meaning to enumerate (الإحصاء); that is, due to his love for wealth and his infatuation with amassing it, he gathers wealth and counts it over and over again, taking pleasure in its increase. It is also said the meaning is that he prepares it as a provision (عدة) and a treasure for the calamities of time.

His saying: يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مالَهُ أَخْلَدَهُ "He supposes that his wealth will make him immortal," meaning it will grant him eternity in this world and repel death and extinction from him. The past tense is used here to intend the future, as indicated by the context of His saying: "He supposes" (يَحْسَبُ).

This human being, due to his inclination toward the earth and being immersed in long-term hopes, is not content with the wealth that suffices for the needs of his short life and the necessities of his numbered days. Rather, the more his wealth increases, the more his greed increases without end. His outward state suggests that he believes wealth will make him immortal. Due to his instinctive love for survival, he becomes preoccupied with gathering and counting it. The wealth he has gathered and counted, and the self-sufficiency (الاستغناء) he perceives, drive him toward rebellion (الطغيان) and exalting himself over other people, as the Exalted said: كَلَّا إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى "Indeed man becomes rebellious when he considers himself self-sufficient" (96:6-7). This arrogance (الاستكبار) and transgression (التعدي) result in slandering and backbiting.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: "He supposes that his wealth will make him immortal" serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying: "who gathers wealth and counts it," and His saying: "who gathers..." etc., serves as a justification for His saying: "Woe to every slanderer and backbiter."

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا لَيُنْبَذَنَّ فِي الْحُطَمَةِ "No indeed! He will surely be cast into the Crusher." (104:4). This is a rebuff (ردع) of his supposition of immortality through wealth. The lam in "he will surely be cast" (لَيُنْبَذَنَّ) is for an oath (القسم). al-Nabdh (النبذ) is to hurl or throw away. al-Hutamah (الحطمة) is an intensive form of al-hatm (الحطم), which is to break or crush; it also carries the meaning of devouring. It is one of the names of Hell (جهنم), as explained by His subsequent saying: "the kindled Fire of Allah."

The meaning is: he is not made immortal by wealth as he supposes; I swear he shall surely die and be hurled into the Crusher.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحُطَمَةُ "And what will show you what is the Crusher?" (104:5). This is for magnification (تفخيم) and instilling terror (تهويل).

His saying, the Exalted: نارُ اللهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ "It is the kindled Fire of Allah, which overspreads the hearts." (104:6-7). Kindling (إيقاد) the fire is to ignite it. al-Ittila‘ (الاطلاع) and al-tulu‘ (الطلوع) upon something means to oversee it and appear over it. The hearts (الأفئدة) is the plural of fu’ad (فؤاد), which is the heart; what is meant by it in the Quran is the principle of perception and thought in man, which is the human soul (النفس الإنسانية).

The meaning of its "overspreading the hearts" is that it burns the interior of the human being just as it burns his exterior, unlike the fire of this world which only burns the exterior. The Exalted said: وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجارَةُ "whose fuel is humans and stones" (2:24).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّها عَلَيْهِمْ مُؤْصَدَةٌ "Indeed it will be closed in upon them" (104:8). Meaning it is shut tight (مطبقة); there is no exit for them from it and no escape.

His saying, the Exalted: فِي عَمَدٍ مُمَدَّدَةٍ "in long-drawn columns." (104:9). al-‘Amad (العمد) is the plural of ‘amud (عمود), meaning column or pillar. al-Tamdīd (التمديد) is an intensive form of stretching (المد). It is said: these are the stakes of the covers that are closed over the people of the Fire. It is also said: they are long columns to which they are bound, like stocks (المقاطر)—which are large pieces of wood or trunks with holes in which the legs of prisoners, such as thieves and others, are placed. Other interpretations have also been mentioned.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Ruh al-Ma‘ani, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَيْلٌ لِكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُمَزَةٍ "Woe to every slanderer and backbiter!": It was revealed—according to what Ibn Abi Hatim extracted via Ibn Ishaq from ‘Uthman ibn ‘Umar—concerning Ubayy ibn Khalaf. And according to what was extracted from al-Suddi, concerning Ubayy ibn ‘Umar al-Thaqafi—famous as al-Akhnas ibn Shariq—for he was a backbiter who frequently attacked people's character.

And according to what Ibn Ishaq said, it was concerning Umayyah ibn Khalaf al-Jumahi—he used to slander the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. And according to what Ibn Jarir and others extracted from Mujahid, it was concerning Jamil ibn ‘Amir. And it was said it was concerning al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah—and his backbiting of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his belittling of him. And it was said concerning al-‘As ibn Wa’il.

I say: He then said: It is possible that it was revealed concerning the group of those mentioned. End of quote. It is not unlikely that these are applications (تطبيق) by the narrators, which is frequent regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَيْلٌ لِكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ "Woe to every slanderer": He said: "The one who makes gestures (يغمز) at people and despises the poor." Regarding His saying: لُمَزَةٍ "backbiter": "He twists his neck and head—and becomes angry when he sees a poor person or a beggar." الَّذِي جَمَعَ مالاً وَعَدَّدَهُ "who gathers wealth and counts it": He said: "He prepared it and set it aside."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ "which overspreads the hearts": He said: "It blazes over the heart." Abu Dharr رضي الله عنه said: "Give tidings to the arrogant of a cauterization in the chests and a dragging upon the backs." Regarding His saying: إِنَّها عَلَيْهِمْ مُؤْصَدَةٌ "Indeed it will be closed in upon them": He said: "Shut tight." فِي عَمَدٍ مُمَدَّدَةٍ "in long-drawn columns": He said: "When the columns are extended over them, by Allah, they shall devour the skins."

In al-Majma‘, al-‘Ayyashi narrated with his chain from Muhammad ibn al-Nu‘man al-Ahwal from Humran ibn A‘yan from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, who said: إن الكفار والمشركين يعيرون أهل التوحيد في النار ـ ويقولون : ما نرى توحيدكم أغنى عنكم شيئا ـ وما نحن وأنتم إلا سواء ـ قال : فيأنف لهم الرب تعالى فيقول للملائكة : اشفعوا فيشفعون لمن شاء الله ـ ثم يقول للنبيين : اشفعوا فيشفعون لمن شاء الله ـ ثم يقول للمؤمنين : اشفعوا فيشفعون لمن شاء الله ـ ويقول الله : أنا أرحم الراحمين اخرجوا برحمتي ـ فيخرجون كما يخرج الفراش. — "The disbelievers and polytheists will taunt the people of monotheism (أهل التوحيد) in the Fire, saying: 'We do not see that your monotheism has availed you anything; we and you are but the same.' He said: 'Then the Lord, the Exalted, will take disdain for them and say to the angels: "Intercede!" So they will intercede for whomsoever Allah wills. Then He will say to the Prophets: "Intercede!" So they will intercede for whomsoever Allah wills. Then He will say to the believers: "Intercede!" So they will intercede for whomsoever Allah wills. Then Allah will say: "I am the Most Merciful of the merciful; come out by My mercy!" So they will come out like moths.'"

He said: Then Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام said: ثم مدت العمد وأوصدت عليهم وكان والله الخلود. — "Then the columns will be extended and it will be closed in upon them, and by Allah, it shall be eternity."
